location.origin: Ivan & Alyosha was formed in 2007 in Seattle, Washington, by Tim Wilson (vocals, guitar) and Ryan Carbary (guitar, vocals). Based in Capitol Hill, Seattle's densest cultural neighborhood and historic center of independent music. Later joined by Pete Wilson (Tim's brother, bass, vocals), Tim Kim (guitar, vocals), and Cole Mauro (drums) for the five-piece configuration. Returned as a duo (Wilson + Carbary) for the 2023 album.
location.formative: Tim Wilson and Ryan Carbary met through Seattle's music community, bonding over shared influences: Americana, folk, pop, and the vocal-harmony tradition (Beatles, Simon & Garfunkel, Byrds). The band name was suggested by producer Eli Thomson during recording of the debut EP in Los Angeles -- named after Ivan and Alyosha Karamazov from Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov (1880). Tim's brother Pete Wilson joined on bass and vocals. Capitol Hill provided the venue infrastructure: Neumos, Barboza, the Comet Tavern, the Egyptian Theatre.
location.career: The Verse, The Chorus EP (2009, self-released, produced by Eli Thomson in LA). Fathers Be Kind EP (2011). All the Times We Had (2013, Dualtone Records -- Nashville independent label, also home to The Lumineers). It's All Just Pretend (2015, Dualtone). Live at Benaroya Hall (2022, with Seattle Symphony, arranged by Lee Mills and Andrew Joslyn). All We Ever Had (2023, Nettwerk Music Group -- Vancouver independent label). KEXP favorites with multiple sessions. Jangly guitars, layered harmonies, upbeat indie folk-pop energy. DATA DISCREPANCY: CSV lists Tender Loving Empire (TLE) as label -- INCORRECT. TLE is a Portland gift shop/label with no Ivan & Alyosha connection. Actual labels: Dualtone Records and Nettwerk Music Group.

labels: Dualtone Records (2013: All the Times We Had; 2015: It's All Just Pretend -- Nashville-based independent label, also home to The Lumineers, The Devil Makes Three, Shovels & Rope; folk-adjacent acts with national reach)
Nettwerk Music Group (2023: All We Ever Had -- Vancouver, BC-based independent label; roster includes Passenger, Old Crow Medicine Show, Angus & Julia Stone; Pacific Northwest indie ecosystem)

members.name: Tim Wilson
members.note: Founding member (2007-present). Brother of Pete Wilson (bass). The primary voice of Ivan & Alyosha -- warm, expressive, earnest. Continues as one half of the duo configuration (2023-present).

members.name: Ryan Carbary
members.note: Founding member (2007-present). Jangly, bright, Byrds-influenced guitar work. Continues as one half of the duo configuration (2023-present). The guitar's contribution to the fraternal jangle.

former_members.name: Pete Wilson
former_members.note: Tim Wilson's brother. The sibling bond -- actual brothers in a band named after fictional brothers. Part of the five-piece lineup through the Dualtone era.

former_members.name: Tim Kim
former_members.note: Additional guitar textures and vocal harmonies in the five-piece configuration.

former_members.name: Cole Mauro
former_members.note: Propulsive drumming in the five-piece configuration.

associated_musicians.name: Eli Thomson
associated_musicians.note: Produced the debut EP The Verse, The Chorus (2009) in Los Angeles. Suggested the band name -- naming them after Ivan and Alyosha Karamazov from Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov. The producer-as-namer: Thomson didn't just record the music, he named the entity. The Dostoevsky reference was Thomson's gift to the band's identity.

associated_musicians.name: Lee Mills
associated_musicians.note: Orchestral arrangements for Live at Benaroya Hall (2022) with the Seattle Symphony.

associated_musicians.name: Andrew Joslyn
associated_musicians.note: Orchestral arrangements for Live at Benaroya Hall (2022). Seattle's indie-classical crossover arranger -- credits include Macklemore ('Same Love,' 'Can't Hold Us'), Mary Lambert, and other Seattle artists bridging indie and institutional contexts.

associated_musicians.name: Seattle Symphony
associated_musicians.note: Live at Benaroya Hall (2022). 2,500-seat concert hall. Folk-pop expanded to symphonic scale.

associated_musicians.name: Fleet Foxes
associated_musicians.note: Fleet Foxes (degree 53, Indie Folk, Sub Pop, E=3) and Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54, Folk, Dualtone/Nettwerk, E=3) are consecutive degrees. Cathedral harmony (five voices as architecture) versus fraternal jangle (two voices as warmth). Both literary: Bruegel versus Dostoevsky. Both E=3.

associated_musicians.name: Brandi Carlile
associated_musicians.note: Carlile (degree 52, Folk/Rock, E=4) recorded at Bear Creek Studios. Ivan & Alyosha performed at Benaroya Hall. Both connect to Seattle's highest institutional music infrastructure.

associated_musicians.name: Grand Archives
associated_musicians.note: Grand Archives (degree 51, Indie Folk, Sub Pop, E=3) and Ivan & Alyosha share the multi-voice harmony aesthetic. Communal harmony (full band organism) versus fraternal jangle (duo intimacy).

associated_musicians.name: Rocky Votolato
associated_musicians.note: Votolato (degree 46, Folk, E=3) and brother Cody co-founded Waxwing. Tim and Pete Wilson are brothers in Ivan & Alyosha. Sibling bonds as creative foundation across Seattle's folk zone.

associated_musicians.name: Shawn Smith
associated_musicians.note: Smith (degree 41, Capitol Hill) and Ivan & Alyosha share the same neighborhood. Capitol Hill's second appearance in the engine.

theory_nodes.concept: The fraternal jangle (fifty-fifth acoustic element -- the bright, ringing, optimistic harmonic texture produced when two voices and two guitars create warmth that is simultaneously musically simple and emotionally complex; the specific quality that occurs when intimately familiar voices -- brothers, lifelong collaborators -- produce harmonies whose emotional resonance exceeds their technical complexity)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- Tim Wilson and Ryan Carbary's vocal harmonies are simpler, brighter, more direct than Fleet Foxes' cathedral density. Two friends singing together, their voices comfortable in each other's range. Jangly guitar arpeggios (Byrds-influenced, ringing open strings, major-key voicings) provide the harmonic foundation. The listener feels invited rather than overwhelmed.

Contrast with the cathedral harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- voices stacked to architectural density, five people building a structure of sound. The fraternal jangle is two people remembering why they started. The cathedral is overwhelming; the jangle is inviting.

Contrast with the communal harmony (degree 51, Grand Archives) -- multiple voices blending into one warm organism. The fraternal jangle is a duo whose intimacy produces warmth. The communal is horizontal (voices spread); the fraternal is diagonal (voices leaning on each other).
Contrast with the testimony roar (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- one voice at maximum power. The fraternal jangle is two voices at maximum warmth. Different modes of presence.

The 'fraternal' modifier captures the sibling element: Tim and Pete Wilson as brothers in the band, the Dostoevsky brothers in the name, the fraternal quality of the sound itself -- music that sounds like it comes from people who know each other deeply. The jangle is the guitar's contribution; the fraternal is the relationship's contribution.

Ten-element folk-acoustic taxonomy: murmur (45), confessional (46), parable tone (47), held rest (48), lullaby subversion (49), intimate architecture (50), communal harmony (51), testimony roar (52), cathedral harmony (53), fraternal jangle (54). Ten modes of folk-rooted sound across ten consecutive degrees.
theory_nodes.description: The fraternal jangle is the fifty-fifth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the bright, ringing, optimistic harmonic texture that occurs when two intimately familiar voices and two guitars create warmth whose emotional resonance exceeds its technical complexity. Ivan & Alyosha's jangly folk-pop defines this phenomenon: Byrds-influenced arpeggios, major-key voicings, and vocal harmonies that sound like trust made audible. The element extends the folk-acoustic taxonomy to ten elements across ten consecutive degrees.

ancestors: The cathedral harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- voices as architecture. The fraternal jangle is voices as warmth.
The communal harmony (degree 51, Grand Archives) -- multiple voices as one organism. The fraternal jangle is a duo as intimacy.
The testimony roar (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- solo voice at maximum power. The fraternal jangle is two voices at maximum warmth.
The murmur (degree 45, Damien Jurado) -- whispered solo intimacy. The fraternal jangle is bright duo warmth.
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-five acoustic elements across fifty-five degrees. The fraternal jangle extends the folk-acoustic taxonomy to ten elements (45-54). Track element 56 at degree 55.

theory_nodes.concept: The neighborhood anchor (fifty-fifth artist-city pattern -- the artist whose identity is mapped to a specific neighborhood within a city, and whose creative life is shaped by that neighborhood's density, culture, venues, and infrastructure; the artist who is rooted, local, embedded at the street level)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- Capitol Hill, Seattle. The neighborhood's venues (Neumos, Barboza, the Comet Tavern, the Egyptian Theatre), record shops, and cafes form the physical infrastructure of the band's creative life. Ivan & Alyosha are not a 'Seattle band' in the abstract; they are a Capitol Hill band.
Contrast with the prodigy return (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- requires departure and return. The neighborhood anchor never leaves because the neighborhood IS the creative context.
Contrast with the rural breakout (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- countryside to city infrastructure. The neighborhood anchor is already in the city's densest cultural zone.
Contrast with the serial founder (degree 51, Grand Archives) -- builds across the city. The neighborhood anchor is rooted in one district.
Capitol Hill is to Seattle's indie folk-pop scene what Sub Pop is to its label ecosystem: the institutional amplifier at the street level.
theory_nodes.description: The neighborhood anchor is the fifty-fifth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the artist whose identity is mapped to a specific neighborhood, not just a city, and whose creative life is shaped by that neighborhood's density, culture, and infrastructure. Ivan & Alyosha's Capitol Hill identity exemplifies the pattern: embedded in the venue cluster, cafe culture, and street-level independent music ecosystem of Seattle's densest cultural neighborhood.

ancestors: The prodigy return (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- suburb to city to world to city. The neighborhood anchor stays in the neighborhood.
The rural breakout (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- countryside to city. The neighborhood anchor is already central.
The serial founder (degree 51, Grand Archives) -- builds across the city. The neighborhood anchor is rooted in one district.
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-five artist-city patterns across fifty-five degrees. The neighborhood anchor introduces street-level specificity. Track pattern 56 at degree 55.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the borrowed name (fifty-fourth deployment; the blue note that arrives when an artist carries a name from somewhere else, a literary identity given rather than chosen, that creates a permanent gap between the band and its identifier; the weight of Dostoevsky carried into three-minute folk-pop)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- Named after characters from Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov (1880). Ivan is the intellectual skeptic; Alyosha is the spiritual believer. The band plays bright, jangly folk-pop. The weight difference between a 900-page meditation on the existence of God and a three-minute pop song is the ache. The blue note plays whether the band intends it or not.

Eli Thomson suggested the name -- a producer in LA naming a Seattle folk-pop band after 19th-century Russian literary characters. The borrowed name is a gift that comes with obligations the recipient may not fully understand.

Contrast with the ancient longing (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- temporal displacement, homesickness for a time never lived. The borrowed name is nominal displacement -- the gap between the name and the named.
Contrast with the survivor's crack (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- present-tense survival. The borrowed name is atemporal: the literary weight exists independently of the band's biography.

Ten consecutive folk-adjacent blue notes (45-54): held breath, crack, suspended resolve, drummer's ghost, Trojan lullaby, submerged current, afterglow, survivor's crack, ancient longing, borrowed name. Ten modes of absence, misdirection, and hidden motion across ten degrees.
theory_nodes.description: The borrowed name is the fifty-fourth blue note deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the blue note that arrives when an artist carries a literary identity that exceeds the carrier -- Dostoevsky's dialectic between faith and doubt encoded in the name of a jangly folk-pop band. The gap between the weight of the name and the lightness of the music is the ache. The borrowed name follows ten consecutive deployments in the folk-adjacent register.

ancestors: Blue note -- the ancient longing (degree 53, Fleet Foxes): temporal displacement, Bruegel cover. The borrowed name is nominal displacement, Dostoevsky title.
Blue note -- the survivor's crack (degree 52, Brandi Carlile): present-tense survival. The borrowed name is atemporal literary weight.
Blue note -- the afterglow (degree 51, Grand Archives): warmth fading. The borrowed name is weight persisting.
Blue note -- the held breath (degree 45, Damien Jurado): silence before sound. The borrowed name is the sound of carrying someone else's meaning.
All fifty-three prior blue note deployments (degrees 0-53)
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-four deployments across fifty-five degrees. Ten consecutive deployments (45-54) map absence, misdirection, and hidden motion in the folk-adjacent register. Track deployment 55 at degree 55.

theory_nodes.concept: Ten consecutive folk-adjacent degrees (Genre Stage 35 -- Folk; EXTENDS the record set at degree 53; Folk at 45, Folk at 46, Indie Folk at 47, Folk at 48, Folk Punk at 49, Indie Folk at 50, Indie Folk at 51, Folk/Rock at 52, Indie Folk at 53, Folk at 54; the folk-adjacent decad; DOUBLE DIGITS)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- Folk. Jangly guitars, layered vocal harmonies, upbeat folk-pop energy. Byrds-influenced arpeggios. KEXP favorites. Dualtone Records (Nashville indie). Capitol Hill. The tenth production paradigm in the folk-adjacent run.

Preceding: Folk at 45 (Jurado), Folk at 46 (Votolato), Indie Folk at 47 (Pedro the Lion), Folk at 48 (Cahoone), Folk Punk at 49 (Dawson), Indie Folk at 50 (Mirah), Indie Folk at 51 (Grand Archives), Folk/Rock at 52 (Carlile), Indie Folk at 53 (Fleet Foxes). Folk at 54 extends the run to ten.

Five Folk within the decad (45, 46, 48, 54) -- wait, correction: five pure Folk at 45, 46, 48, and now 54 (four), plus four Indie Folk at 47, 50, 51, 53. Plus one Folk Punk (49) and one Folk/Rock (52). Folk is the most common specific genre at four of ten degrees.
DOUBLE DIGITS. Ten consecutive. No other genre family has achieved this in the engine.
Track whether folk-adjacent extends to degree 55 (eleven) or the engine finally shifts genre.
theory_nodes.description: Folk at degree 54 establishes Genre Stage 35, extending the folk-adjacent run to ten consecutive degrees. The record set at degree 53 (nine) now extends to double digits. The folk-adjacent decad (45-54) is the longest genre-family streak in the engine's documented history. Ivan & Alyosha is the fifth pure Folk within the decad (joining 45, 46, 48), making Folk the most common specific sub-genre at four of ten degrees.

ancestors: Indie Folk at degree 53 (Fleet Foxes) -- Genre Stage 34. Folk at 54 extends to Stage 35.
Nine consecutive folk-adjacent at degrees 45-53 -- Genre Stages 27-34. Now ten consecutive.
Swallow quintet -- original record for longest same-family run. BROKEN at six (degree 50). Extended to seven (51), eight (52), nine (53), ten (54).
genealogy.relationship: Genre Stage 35 at degree 54. Ten consecutive folk-adjacent degrees (45-54). Extends record set at 53. DOUBLE DIGITS. Track whether folk-adjacent extends to degree 55.

theory_nodes.concept: E=3 rises to thirteen deployments and TIES E=4 (thirteenth E=3 deployment; energy distribution 2:16:13:13:10:2; E=3 equals E=4 at 13 each; folk zone energy 2-3-3-2-3-2-3-4-3-3)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- E=3. KEXP favorites. Dualtone Records (Nashville indie, Lumineers label). Benaroya Hall with Seattle Symphony. National independent distribution. Consistent touring. Meaningful regional and indie visibility without the critical mass of Fleet Foxes' Pitchfork #1 or Carlile's Grammy dominance.
E=3 TIES E=4 at 13 each. First time E=3 and E=4 have been equal since the early engine. E=2 still leads at 16.
Same E=3 as degrees 46 (Votolato), 47 (Pedro the Lion), 49 (Dawson), 51 (Grand Archives), and 53 (Fleet Foxes). Six of ten folk-adjacent degrees at E=3.
Folk zone energy pattern across ten degrees: E=2, E=3, E=3, E=2, E=3, E=2, E=3, E=4, E=3, E=3. Ten degrees. One E=4 spike. Three at E=2. Six at E=3.
Ivan & Alyosha is the lower end of E=3 -- more visible than E=2 but less acclaimed than Fleet Foxes' universal critical consensus.
theory_nodes.description: E=3 at degree 54 is the thirteenth E=3 deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. Energy distribution after degree 54: E1:2, E2:16, E3:13, E4:13, E5:10, E6:2. E=3 TIES E=4 at 13 each -- the first time these two energy levels have been equal. Ivan & Alyosha is E=3 because the band has meaningful indie visibility (KEXP, Dualtone/Nettwerk, Benaroya Hall) without mainstream critical mass. Six of ten folk-adjacent degrees are E=3.

ancestors: E=3 at degree 53 (Fleet Foxes): Pitchfork #1, universal acclaim. E=3 at 54 is lower-end E=3 -- same energy bracket, different scale of acclaim.
E=4 at degree 52 (Brandi Carlile): nine Grammys, Super Bowl. E=3 at 54 continues the folk zone's return to native register.
E=3 at degrees 46-47, 49, 51 (Votolato, Pedro the Lion, Dawson, Grand Archives): similar indie-visible profiles.
genealogy.relationship: Thirteen E=3 deployments across fifty-five degrees. E=3 TIES E=4 at 13. Six of ten folk-adjacent degrees are E=3. Folk zone energy: 2-3-3-2-3-2-3-4-3-3. Track at degree 55.

theory_nodes.concept: First literary-named band in the engine (Ivan & Alyosha named after characters from Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov, 1880; suggested by producer Eli Thomson; the intellectual skeptic and the spiritual believer; the dialectic of faith and doubt carried in a band name)
examples: Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- named after Ivan Karamazov (intellectual skeptic, argues against God) and Alyosha Karamazov (novice monk, embodies compassion). The Brothers Karamazov (1880) is Dostoevsky's final novel -- a 900-page meditation on faith, doubt, suffering, and free will. The band plays three-minute jangly folk-pop.

Producer Eli Thomson suggested the name during recording of The Verse, The Chorus EP in LA. The producer-as-namer: Thomson shaped both the debut sound and the band's permanent identity. The Dostoevsky reference was Thomson's gift.
The ampersand holds the dialectic: Ivan (doubt) & Alyosha (faith). The band never resolves the tension; they carry it.

No prior degree references literary fiction in naming. The engine has encountered biographical names, descriptive names (Fleet Foxes, Grand Archives), and geographic references, but never a direct literary allusion.
Album titles continue the literary-textual consciousness: The Verse, The Chorus (meta-musical); All the Times We Had (memorial); It's All Just Pretend (philosophical); All We Ever Had (elegiac).
theory_nodes.description: Ivan & Alyosha is the first band in the Seattle 360 engine named after literary characters -- Ivan and Alyosha Karamazov from Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov (1880). The name was suggested by producer Eli Thomson. The literary naming introduces a new dimension to the engine: a band that carries the weight of a 19th-century Russian novel about faith, doubt, and brotherhood in its title. The dialectic between reason (Ivan) and faith (Alyosha) is the novel's central tension and the band's permanent inheritance.

ancestors: Fleet Foxes (degree 53) -- Pieter Bruegel the Elder cover art. Visual art reference. Ivan & Alyosha introduces literary fiction reference.
Grand Archives (degree 51) -- descriptive name (an archive of sounds). Ivan & Alyosha is a character reference.
Pedro the Lion (degree 47) -- Pedro references children's literature. Ivan & Alyosha references Dostoevsky. Different literary registers.
genealogy.relationship: First literary-named band at degree 54. New engine category: bands named after fictional characters. Track whether more literary references appear.

theory_nodes.concept: Capitol Hill returns -- second appearance (degree 41 Shawn Smith, degree 54 Ivan & Alyosha; thirteen degrees apart; Seattle's densest cultural neighborhood and historic center of independent music)
examples: Shawn Smith (degree 41) -- Capitol Hill's first appearance. Rock/Alternative. Brad, Satchel, Pigeonhed.
Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- Capitol Hill's second appearance. Folk. Jangly folk-pop. Thirteen degrees later.

Capitol Hill is Seattle's indie music center: Neumos, Barboza, the Comet Tavern, the Egyptian Theatre. The neighborhood where the venues cluster, the cafes congregate, the independent music infrastructure concentrates.
Two appearances, thirteen degrees apart, two different genres (Rock/Alternative vs Folk). Capitol Hill hosts everything.
theory_nodes.description: Capitol Hill appears for the second time at degree 54 (Ivan & Alyosha), thirteen degrees after its first appearance at degree 41 (Shawn Smith). Capitol Hill is Seattle's densest cultural neighborhood -- the historic center of independent music, LGBTQ+ community, and nightlife. Two different genres mapped to the same neighborhood confirm Capitol Hill's genre-agnostic cultural gravity.

ancestors: Shawn Smith (degree 41) -- Capitol Hill's first appearance. Rock/Alternative context.
Sand Point to Wedgwood (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- NE Seattle. Capitol Hill is central Seattle. Different neighborhoods, different cultural densities.
genealogy.relationship: Capitol Hill: second appearance at degree 54. Thirteen degrees between appearances (41 and 54). Track future Capitol Hill appearances.

seattle_connection: THE FRATERNAL JANGLE -- Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) connects to Seattle as the neighborhood anchor: rooted in CAPITOL HILL, Seattle's densest cultural neighborhood and historic center of independent music. Formed 2007 by TIM WILSON (vocals, guitar) and RYAN CARBARY (guitar, vocals). Named after characters from Dostoevsky's THE BROTHERS KARAMAZOV -- suggested by producer ELI THOMSON in Los Angeles. Five-piece through Dualtone era: Tim Wilson, Carbary, PETE WILSON (Tim's brother, bass, vocals), TIM KIM (guitar, vocals), COLE MAURO (drums). THE VERSE, THE CHORUS EP (2009, self-released, produced by Eli Thomson in LA). FATHERS BE KIND EP (2011). ALL THE TIMES WE HAD (2013, DUALTONE RECORDS -- Nashville indie, also home to The Lumineers). IT'S ALL JUST PRETEND (2015, Dualtone). LIVE AT BENAROYA HALL (2022, with SEATTLE SYMPHONY, LEE MILLS & ANDREW JOSLYN -- 2,500-seat concert hall, folk-pop at symphonic scale). ALL WE EVER HAD (2023, NETTWERK MUSIC GROUP -- Vancouver). Returned as TWO-PIECE (Wilson + Carbary). DATA DISCREPANCY: CSV says Tender Loving Empire -- INCORRECT. Actual labels Dualtone and Nettwerk. KEXP FAVORITES -- multiple sessions. Jangly guitars, layered harmonies, upbeat indie folk-pop. SIBLING BOND: Tim and Pete Wilson, brothers in a band named after fictional brothers. FIRST LITERARY-NAMED BAND in the engine -- Dostoevsky characters. CAPITOL HILL RETURNS (2nd time after Shawn Smith degree 41). TENTH CONSECUTIVE FOLK-ADJACENT (45-54) -- record extends to TEN. DOUBLE DIGITS. Genre Stage 35 (Folk). E=3 (rises to 13). Distribution 2:16:13:13:10:2. E=3 TIES E=4 at 13 each. THE FRATERNAL JANGLE as 55th acoustic element -- bright, intimate, relational harmony from two voices and two guitars, trust expressed as music. Ten-element folk-acoustic taxonomy (45-54). THE NEIGHBORHOOD ANCHOR as 55th artist-city pattern -- rooted in Capitol Hill's street-level culture and venue infrastructure. THE BORROWED NAME as 54th blue note -- the weight of a literary name that was given, not chosen; Dostoevsky's 900-page dialectic carried into three-minute folk-pop. Gender 14:41 (~25.5%). The engine found a band that named itself after Dostoevsky's arguing brothers, played jangly folk-pop on Capitol Hill, put actual brothers on the same stage, filled Benaroya Hall with the Seattle Symphony, shrank from five to two, and proved that the simplest harmony -- two friends, two guitars, the verse, the chorus -- carries as much weight as a cathedral when the name on the door is borrowed from a novel about the existence of God.

ANDREW JOSLYN: Arranger. Macklemore, Mary Lambert, Ivan & Alyosha. Seattle's indie-classical bridge. Track.
KEXP: Seattle's indie radio. Multiple Ivan & Alyosha sessions. The folk zone's broadcast anchor. Track.

TEN-ELEMENT FOLK-ACOUSTIC TAXONOMY: Murmur (45), confessional (46), parable tone (47), held rest (48), lullaby subversion (49), intimate architecture (50), communal harmony (51), testimony roar (52), cathedral harmony (53), fraternal jangle (54). Complete decad. Track.
